posts.name: Eugene Bergen
posts.cooked: William Kelley is the real Star Wine critic. The real differentiating factor is his amazing objectivity and tasting capability in both old and young wines. His slightly geek profile shows his pure objectivity, because he cant do otherwise, not affected by the broader opinion or politics. William is a wine master who truly “masters” the subject in every aspect.

link_counts.title: Does Bordeaux provide the best QPR wines in the world right now?

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked:
I am assuming we are talking Parker part 1, and not his later, sadder years post 1995. In the early days, I found him extremely useful for Bordeaux, although I learned early not to trust his Burgundy notes.

I think William will prove better than Parker mark 1, as his Burgundy palate is far more refined. But I do worry about the longevity of any critic tasting as many as 100 wines a day.

posts.name: Eugene Bergen
posts.cooked: Interesting as a passionate winelover from Amsterdam (can AI do that?) I am already “blamed” (cant yet take it as a compliment) to actually be AI. Funny how we start to mix real and fake worlds. But at the same time: is this relevant: I would just take a hard look at Kelley’s tasting notes and judge yourself:)

posts.name: Eugene Bergen
posts.cooked: Coult not agree more!

posts.name: John Maguire
posts.cooked:
Apologies for that - the original post just kind of came out of the blue and had an odd ring to it. At any rate, I don’t think anyone can have the same influence as Parker did, and I also think that’s a good thing, although it might be a little more confusing to people new to the sport not to have an easy and universal reference point.

I agree w @Mark_Golodetz in that Parker progressively degenerated in the 1990s. Even in his heyday, I would distinguish between his tasting notes — which helped me understand a wine I hadn’t tasted — and his scores, which often made not much sense below the tasting note. And yes, I never took him seriously on Burgundy, not because I knew (or know) anything, but because I found out early the story of his being “banned” from Burgundy after accusing a domaine (think it was Faiveley) of doctoring their wines on the basis of the flimsiest of evidence. Not the best way to make friends - even worse than calling someone an AI chatbot .

I don’t see a state of the world with Kelley where some celebrities (I can think of one off the top of my head) declare in public that they refuse to drink “anything that isn’t a Parker 100 pt wine.” This is partly the market and partly Kelley’s “geekiness” factor. To me that’s good - people actually, you know, have to read Kelley to get the value of what he is saying. And to me that value is tremendous; it doesn’t hurt that he writes extremely well.

Anyway, sorry again for confusing you with generated text. Glad you appreciate Kelley, as do I.

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked:
JohnMag:

And yes, I never took him seriously on Burgundy, not because I knew (or know) anything, but because I found out early the story of his being “banned” from Burgundy after accusing a domaine (think it was Faiveley) of doctoring their wines on the basis of the flimsiest of evidence

Yes, it was Faiveley, and of course the story goes a lot deeper than that. Parker wrote that a wine that he tasted in bottle did not taste the same as the one he had at the Domaine. I don’t think there was an issue with that. He did add “Hmm!” which implied that it was deliberate. Francois Faiveley took exception and sued. The big problem was of course, the case was going to be tried in France, with different laws, and rules of evidence . For instance, I seem to remember there is a pretty arcane law in France concerning disparaging a product, and Parker did not want to risk a major loss, so settled.

(BTW, cattle farmers in Texas tried to pull the same kind of stunt with Oprah and lost. https://www.washingtonpost.com/archive/politics/1998/02/27/oprah-winfrey-wins-case-filed-by-cattlemen/dd4612f5-ccbf-4e3d-a1c1-f84d1f4fd23c/).

I have had the same experience of tasting very different wines at the property and then the same wine in the US. It is a reason why critics should taste in bottle a couple of years after their initial notes. Barrel tastings are a minefield anyway, and although primeur tasting is way more transparent than before, I still hear stories where barrel samples are a little or sometimes a lot better than the finished wine. Most wines around 2000 did malolactic in tank, but a few quite openly did it in barrel to “give a clearer idea of the wine’s potential.” Hmm!

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
Eugene_Bergen:

Interesting as a passionate winelover from Amsterdam (can AI do that?) I am already “blamed” (cant yet take it as a compliment) to actually be AI. Funny how we start to mix real and fake worlds. But at the same time: is this relevant: I would just take a hard look at Kelley’s tasting notes and judge yourself:)

I think I understand what you were trying to say. It also looks like you have not posted here in about three years. In the past three years William Kelley has been highly active and a major contributor to this wine forum. I think opinions of him on this board are generally quite favorable. I actually re-subscribed to the Robert Parker website just because of him. I think I ended my subscription of Parker back around 1999, so it was quite the paradigm shift for me. My palate aligns with his quite well in Bordeaux, Beaujolais (although he can handle the riper years better than me), and Northern Rhone.

posts.name: Greg Kahn
posts.cooked:
Mark_Golodetz:
JohnMag:

And yes, I never took him seriously on Burgundy, not because I knew (or know) anything, but because I found out early the story of his being “banned” from Burgundy after accusing a domaine (think it was Faiveley) of doctoring their wines on the basis of the flimsiest of evidence

Yes, it was Faiveley, and of course the story goes a lot deeper than that. Parker wrote that a wine that he tasted in bottle did not taste the same as the one he had at the Domaine. I don’t think there was an issue with that. He did add “Hmm!” which implied that it was deliberate. Francois Faiveley took exception and sued. The big problem was of course, the case was going to be tried in France, with different laws, and rules of evidence . For instance, I seem to remember there is a pretty arcane law in France concerning disparaging a product, and Parker did not want to risk a major loss, so settled.

(BTW, cattle farmers in Texas tried to pull the same kind of stunt with Oprah and lost. https://www.washingtonpost.com/archive/politics/1998/02/27/oprah-winfrey-wins-case-filed-by-cattlemen/dd4612f5-ccbf-4e3d-a1c1-f84d1f4fd23c/).

I have had the same experience of tasting very different wines at the property and then the same wine in the US. It is a reason why critics should taste in bottle a couple of years after their initial notes. Barrel tastings are a minefield anyway, and although primeur tasting is way more transparent than before, I still hear stories where barrel samples are a little or sometimes a lot better than the finished wine. Most wines around 2000 did malolactic in tank, but a few quite openly did it in barrel to “give a clearer idea of the wine’s potential.” Hmm!

Also, while I have no comment on whether Faiveley did anything untoward with respect to the wines he presented to Parker, those weren’t very good wines either way, and ironically made towards a style Parker liked (enough oak to make them hard to tell apart from St. Estephe blind).

posts.name: Julian Marshall
posts.cooked:
I agree that he’s the best of the current crop. I’m not in the market for EP, but I like the way he spends so much time in the area, retasting several times before making his mind up. I also like the fact that he tries to reduce score inflation. His writing is much better than most of his peers and he has technical knowledge which sets him apart. Of course, that he actually makes wine himself is another plus point - it isn’t a prerequisite to be a good wine writer, but it must help understand the challenges faced by wineries.
But I also agree with others that he is unlikely to ever have the hegemony of Parker, and it’s just as well.

posts.name: Jim Stewart
posts.cooked: He’s okay.

posts.name: Tom Blach
posts.cooked: In 1993(that is the vintage in question as I understand it) Faiveley made wines which I have loved almost more than anything else I’ve ever drunk. Not that I’m claiming the authority of objectivity, of course.

posts.name: David Cooper
posts.cooked:
William is great but, can he overcome the poor technology that is used to present his work online? Especially when compared to the much better Vinous site.

I pay for both but rarely go to the WA. Cellartracker always leads me to Vinous seamlessly. Finding Dr Kelly’s notes are harder but, worth the effort. I also find the WA articles too short, like the ones in Time magazine.

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked: I remember when I first read that from Parker I thought he was saying that the wine was poorly shipped from France to the US - not uncommon, esp. in those days. It was only later that I learned that others thought the comment was more disparaging than that.

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked:
I really like William Kelley writing and also resubscribed to the Wine Advocate after not doing so for many years because of him.

Two questions about the OP’s comment. I don’t like the word “the”. As others have said, while William is fabulous, there are today a number of excellent wine writers and it would be more accurate to say that William is a worthy successor to Robert Parker.

Second, also as others have said, I don’t believe there is such a thing as an objective tasting note, nor should there be. When a wine writer says his tasting notes are objective, I think they are being arrogant and saying their palate is the right one and everyone else’s is wrong. I don’t remember William ever making such an arrogant suggestion.

Second, also as others have said, I don’t believe there is such a thing as an objective tasting note, nor should there be. posts.name: Pat_Martin
posts.cooked: Maybe mentioned already, but I think John Gilman is also great overall. He doesn’t review a lot of wines I look for, but when he does he’s notes are both informative and unfailing for my palate especially for whites and bubbly. When I see scores listed with a wine, it’s Kelley and Gilman that I look for (I do use Dunnuck as a reverse indicator sometimes).

posts.name: Craig Gleason

I like William’s notes and articles, and it’s great that he’s really exploring the impact of farming on the wines. Amazing to have his interaction here as well.

posts.name: RH Drexel
posts.cooked: I’m a fan of William’s and got to hang out with him a bit when he was in Santa Barbara. Very talented young man with a great palate.

posts.name: Donald Pennet
posts.cooked:
I have been taking a break, my mental health . I love William Kelley the wine geek, I hate what he does with prices, with champagne he is too partisan and does not cover enough to be a great independent critic.

I also feel like all critics these days, he is too much in love with the luxury side and for this reason there will only ever be one Robert Parker. Also,no other critic could convey the gist of wine in simple language like Parker could. Agree with his taste or disagree with his taste, that is another matter.

reply_to_user.name: Phil T r o t t i e r

posts.name: Robert Dentice
posts.cooked:
Comparing critics from different eras is like comparing athletes it is impossible. Today there are so many more wines, critics, technology, amateur reviewers, etc. that no one will ever have the singular influence Parker had.

Having said that William is the only reviewer who can move markets in a way that was similar to Parker. I just got back from a quick trip to Burgundy and there was much talk about his influence. Let me be clear there is universal respect for his knowledge and passion and even praise for his personal wine project. What is troubling is his impact on prices. I think of all current critics he has the best combination of in-depth knowledge, work ethic, tech savvy, unrelenting drive and its obvious he is a true wine geek. I have zero idea why he is still at the Wine Advocate. ZERO. He could easily have his own publication and my quess is he will at some point.

posts.name: Andreas Scheppler
posts.cooked:
I would argue that he is moving prices in Burgundy (think Lamy) and Champagne (think U. Collin, Egly) like no one before him and on a scale that can only be compared to Bob’s hey day influence in Bordeaux.

The only question for me is whether he will have the same impact in Bordeaux. So far the answer is no, but I wouldn’t be surprised if he did in a few years’ time.

Why is that? Because a) he’s not style-agnostic like so many critics (and so few consumers), b) he doesn’t care about hierarchy, and c) he’s not afraid to call out bad wines (by big names).

posts.name: Rick Allen
posts.cooked: Part of the problem with Burgundy and Champagne is that the supply of individual wines is limited. If a dozen people decide to buy a specific bottling due to WK’s comments, it can move the market in many instances. It takes a lot more people to move the market in Bordeaux.

posts.name: Andreas Scheppler
posts.cooked:
Rick_Allen:

Part of the problem with Burgundy and Champagne is that the supply of individual wines is limited. If a dozen people decide to buy a specific bottling due to WK’s comments, it can move the market in many instances. It takes a lot more people to move the market in Bordeaux.

True. But for the “heir of Parker” discussion it’s not that relevant: supply was always that limited but nobody had an impact like Kelley. I agree Bordeaux prices will never triple (or tenfold like Lamy), but my guess is that he will move prices like nobdy since Parker.

I don’t know about the successor thing though I understand the praise. He’s his own thing, and I think Robert captured it quite well.

Starting to make wines yourself and being a wine critic is something I find fascinating. It both forces you to thing about details a critic rarely considers, and I’m sure it also humbles creating an interesting balance. And brings a depth to everything around wine.

posts.name: Richard Frankel
posts.cooked:
Having only chatted with William online, I bet he is flattered by comparisons to Parker. Love or hate him, Parker was (and maybe still is) the most famous/successful English language wine critic of the last 50 years. To be the next Parker means extreme success, and William, despite his modest demeanor, would not scorn that.

I am a big fan of William for all the reasons discussed in this thread. One might argue that Galloni is - so far - the current successor to Parker in terms of moving the market (see Burlotto) but perhaps more importantly, in building the most widely influential (English language) wine criticism publication of the current era.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Hi Howard!

It is not so much being smart, as not knowing where to start…

On the one hand, I’m obviously flattered by the compliments I’ve been paid in this thread. And I certainly wouldn’t want my reticence to be mistaken for indifference: I’m sincerely grateful to anyone who takes an interest in my work, and more than delighted if that work is enriching their wine experience.

Equally, however, I feel a little diffident. I have never sought, or had the pretension, to position myself as “the next” RP, and I cannot and do not claim any kind of apostolic succession: we have met a few times, and exchanged a few emails, but no more than that. I am also very wary of what the sociologist Dan Sperber calls the “guru effect”: I hope that I scrupulously avoid arguments from authority in my reviews, and try to give the reasoning why I esteem particular producers and wines in such a way that those with differing priorities and preferences will find the means with which to disagree with me in the reviews themselves. And while I certainly aspire to give candid reviews without fear or favor (I prefer that phrase to “objective”, but let’s not get into philosophy), I am acutely conscious, as a wine producer in my spare time, that no judgement is infallible in wine. This is why I value the Donald Pennets of this world, cantankerous and unreasonable though they may sometimes be: if wine is a matter of taste, excessive consensus in wine should make us all uneasy.

I do admire, and draw inspiration from, much of RP’s body of work. Above all, his willingness to disrupt traditional/established hierarchies and taste the wine for what it is seems more relevant than ever today, as wine writing becomes harder and harder to distinguish from advertorial, and it’s all too easy to name the winners in advance. No influential voice has an unmitigatedly positive influence, but I tend to think that we take for granted a lot of the positives of RP’s influence: who else dared to give great reviews to the wines of the Mâconnais, or a young guy named Anselme Selosse, or the supposedly rustic country wines of Cornas, in the early to mid 1980s? Those reviews in some cases changed the landscape of wine, saving vineyards that might have become vacation houses for wealthy Lyonnais. Then there’s his Stakhanovite work ethic, which I certainly can’t match.

But I also think that wine writing needs to evolve. What do I aspire to achieve? I want to bring deeper context and depth to wine reviews. At its most elementary, that simply means visiting the producers: I learnt wine criticism tasting in the Côte d’Or, where you have to visit everyone; it was only later that I discovered that most other wine regions were reviewed in massed comparative tastings. I try to review all the regions I cover the way I review the Côte d’Or: visiting the producers, understanding their practices (for better or worse). This is not about simply hearing what they have to say and repeating it; and indeed some producers in Bordeaux, where it was always an axiom that a site visit would equal a better score, have been perplexed that I am liable to visit their vineyards but also not like their wines. Instead, it’s about verifying the coherence of the discourse, the tasting, and the actuality on the ground.

I also hope to bridge what I perceive as a widening gap between producers (and by producers I mean the people who actually make the wines, so not always the proprietors) and consumers. My article on Bordeaux’s “state of the art” was an attempt to do this. Farming and making some wine of my own has given me some insight into production, and a few ways to break the ice and get people to speak candidly about what they do. The corollary is that I hope to foreground the important of great agronomy and technique (in the sense that one might compliment a musician’s technique) in wine production. A variety of reasons mean that great farming, even in the greatest appellations, is rarer than it should be; and I think wine writers should know the difference between a vineyard lavished with 1,000 hours per hectare manual work by a salaried team vs one that has seen 300 hours per hectare of subcontracted labor. I should probably go further on this subject, especially in the Côte d’Or, and it’s a project I have in mind.

I also tend to think we are moving away from an era when people sought simplification of the complex to where they seek to understand more deeply—at least in this very particular niche. I try to regularly remind myself that the raison d’être of a wine review is to explain why the wine should be of interest, and if possible to explain how it got to taste the way it tastes. I’m not trying to offer any short cuts, but rather (and I hope without being too élitist), offer insights into the complex questions. For me, the real potential “competitors” I think about are passionate (and, for my regions, wealthy) consumers who try to get to the heart of what makes a given wine interesting. I hope to be able to bring something to the table that even people who can drink these wines every night can’t get, by going deeper into the process behind them.

Anyway, these are just a couple of my thoughts about the big and complex subject that is the future of wine writing. Thanks once again to everyone who had something to say; it’s interesting/flattering/salutary to read. And if I don’t do much in the way of follow-up posting, it’s because I’m a little behind with my Champagne report!

All best,

William.

posts.cooked: Am I right in thinking that your French is better than Parker’s, so site visits are probably much more complete and insightful than his. I know he did speak French but not fluently.

posts.cooked:
[quote=“William_Kelley, post:65, topic:305657”]
******…I hope that I scrupulously avoid arguments from authority in my reviews, and try to give the reasoning why I esteem particular producers and wines in such a way that those with differing priorities and preferences will find the means with which to disagree with me in the reviews themselves. And while I certainly aspire to give candid reviews without fear or favor (I prefer that phrase to “objective”, but let’s not get into philosophy),[/quote]

Very well said… I adore it, Some random thoughts - specially to my friend : Johon - devoted wine lover. . .

A dream…a quest…or …a…

A Zhao warrior donned his braided hat;
His scimitar gleamed like frost and snow.
His white steed clad in a silver saddle;
As quick as fleeting galloped away.
Within ten steps one foe falls;
Within one thousand miles and none can halt,
When the deed is done;
To a life of anonymity he returns.

Humility,is a first step for a hero - may I say, …

posts.name: Marcu$ Stanley
posts.cooked: William doesn’t have a “burner” account, he posts publicly under his own name, and thoughtfully.

posts.name: Marcu$ Stanley
posts.cooked:
Too many people here are talking about Parker like the late Parker of the oughts is the only Parker that existed. In the 80s and 90s Parker had a revolutionary impact on Bordeaux that reached down into technical winemaking, and often for the better. He also revolutionized the extent of consumer sophistication about Bordeaux. It’s not a negative thing to compare a critic to that version of Parker. One could make some legitimate parallels to Kelley I think, although of course parker never understood Burgundy, and Kelley is operating in a very different and more sophisticated environment in terms of both public wine knowledge and winemaking technique.

From the late 90s on it felt like producers were making wines specifically targeted to the most exaggerated aspects of Parker’s palate and that ended up being quite problematic. But Parker still had a formidable palate for Bordeaux. I often drink aged wines from that era and compare them to Parker’s initial reviews; he’s frequently very on target.
